speaker: interviewer
text: OK

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November the 23rd, 1995. The liberator is Rabbi Leslie Hardman. The interviewer is Bernice Krantz. We're in London, England. And the language is English. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, roll.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Bernice Krantz. It's November the 23rd, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Rabbi Leslie Hardman, and we're in London, England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please, can you tell me your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leslie Henry Hardman. You want me to spell Hardman? H-A-R-D-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you give me your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can-- February the 18th, 1913.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my age at the moment is 62 and a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Right. Can you tell me something of your life as a young man and as a child-- prewar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Having been born in Glenlivet, South Wales-- and it was just at the beginning of the first world war of 1914-- the first world war broke out. Father of [NON-ENGLISH] was an enemy alien-- enemy in inverted commas-- in the sense he was born in Austria-Poland. And he came here at the beginning of the century, or the back end of the last century. And after a while, after a certain number of years, he was advised to become a naturalized citizen. And the-- the papers were set into motion. And then the war broke out. So unfortunately, he-- at that particular time-- he couldn't do anything more, but then to accept that he was an enemy alien. And when he was interned--

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Where was he interned?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was interned in the Isle of Man. That's what I'm told, eventually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he already married at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes, I was born. My two sisters were born. Yes, my other brother and third sister were born in Manchester. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So eventually, we, as our mother decided-- so she took us to-- to Manchester. She had very little money, and fortunately-- perhaps her brothers-- who were living in Liverpool, or one was in Manchester-- helped. And-- and that's when-- so we grew up there. The war-- the war ended.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall having missed my father, or anything was said about my relationship with him, or his relationship with us. We just-- well, life went on. And then we moved to another little street in Manchester. I think we moved originally in 1916-- 15-- 1916, I was two or three at the time. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they helped. The family helped him. And then we moved to Bellevue-- Bellevue-- near the-- Bellevue-- a beautiful zoo and so on. And I don't think we-- there were many Jews living at that particular time in that area. Anyhow, so I-- yes, how old was I? Six? Seven? And your father was back with you by this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was back. Yes, he is back. He was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he was doing cabinet making or wood turning-- associated with wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a religious man?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a religious man, but not-- how can you say religious? Well, a kosher home? We keep Shabbas at home, but then he had to work on Shabbas. And-- yes, then I-- then I-- then I was sent to a non-Jewish school not far from where we lived. And I don't know whether it was a religious school or not, but I remember being beaten across the back for not kneeling. That upset my parents. I can't remember whether it upset me, to be honest. Anyhow, they decided we should-- I should leave that school and go to the Jewish school, which is in Cheetham Hill Road, which meant taking two tram cars-- 34 tram car and 32-- what a memory. And I had to get up very early. But nonetheless, in spite of that, I won a special prize afterwards-- after a few years-- for punctuality and regularity. And that also covered Sunday morning. Mm-hmm. They gave me a beautiful book. What a memory I've got-- some things I can-- by Spenser-- a very-- it's a classical book. But to me, it was like-- it was as if it was like a childish book. I didn't want it. So I got another book by G.A. Henty. Ooh, I can't remember the name. Anyhow, so we lived there until 1923-- yes, 1923. Then we went to Liverpool. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then I went to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the Jewish school, Jewish day school, and then to the yeshiva. And I was always interested in religion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Whether one of my teachers seemed to inspire me, I don't know. But-- and apparently, I wasn't a great term academic individual from the point of view of-- of knowledge generally. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Yes. So I went to the yeshiva. And I wanted to stay on. They left school at 14. I'm-- I passed the exam for-- for the scholarship at the first go, but we had to have recall-- a recall. And I failed the recall. I continued with yeshiva learning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then time went on. I was 17, 18-- what's gonna happen to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Roshashiva said, let him study for [NON-ENGLISH]. The study was pretty heavy-- and comprehensive-- and I passed.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the funniest thing was when the [NON-ENGLISH] said, we can't give you Kabbalah, which is the equivalent title like, [NON-ENGLISH] for rabbi, Kabbalah for [NON-ENGLISH]. We can't give it you until you get a job. And when I applied for jobs, said, we can't give it you until you get Kabbalah. That was the first time I got angry with the so-called establishment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, eventually, I got it. Eventually, I got it. And I went to St. Annes summer-- a summer place-- St. Annes near-- near Blackpool. And it was pretty boring. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I covered the whole gamut of ministerial work, including shechita. We had 2 or 3 cows a week and about 100 chickens a week. And then we'd come up against, again, the so-called lay establishment of a congregation. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know whether-- I'll put this in if you-- if you have to-- if you have to take it out, you'll take it out. Well, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: We take out nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, know the situation is called glatt kosher-- glatt kosher. Glatt kosher means, in a few words, that when you examine the lungs that there's no adhesions from the lungs to the ribs. If you find an adhesion, you've got to be very careful. You don't snap it quickly. So when you take the lungs out of the beast, and then you blow it up to the extent that-- as it was grow-- as-- as it was when it was in the animal when the animal was alive. You put a little water on it. And if it bubbles, then it's treif. And if it doesn't bubble, then you know it's just perspiration. It wasn't a hole. And the-- through perspiration, and what have you, the adhesions made-- covered the hole. But once it doesn't bubble, it's an indication that it's just perspiration-- nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Unfortunately, the two beasts I killed that week, both were treif. And it was a holiday time. So the president of the community-- wow. What do you want? What kind of a [NON-ENGLISH] are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said here's the telephone. Here's the number. Phone the best in. Obviously, they couldn't do anything about it. Then I-- then I'd realized that there's a subtle difference between the Irish cattle and other cattle. That also applied to liver too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't have a congregation at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was the congregation--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --a small congregation.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you have many jobs within that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had many jobs, yeah. I used to teach the children there. There was a small community. But at the winter-- during the winter-- it was the first winter I stood it. But the second, I said, I'm going home. You don't really need me. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- Were you married at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- no, no, no, no, no. It was there I married-- I met my wife's family-- two-- her two brothers-- very religious, very learned. And I was very impressed with the-- with the-- with the family. That's the kind of family I would like to be associated with in terms of marriage-- not that I was thinking of it. How that came about was her-- her great uncle, I think it was. He was a guest at one of the hotels, and he took ill. And they called me. I didn't know the relationship at that time. I was just there as the minister. And he said to me-- he said, I've got a good shidduch for you. I've come to-- [LAUGHS] to comfort him-- or God forbid, to say the last prayers over him. And he's thinking of a shidduch for me-- very nice. The association was made. And then-- so I got engaged. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But I wasn't at all happy with the idea of getting married and remaining in this particular position. I don't think I earned more than 5 pounds a week-- or was it 4 pounds? And I tried around-- oh, yes, then I soon decided I'm gonna to catch up with my general studies.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went-- studied Latin again--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --and in order to get what you call the equivalent of the matriculation in those days. And then suddenly, the Leeds position was-- became vacant. I accepted that. That was trouble-- do I-- do you need all this? Yeah, it's fine. Carry on.

speaker: interviewee
text: I applied. I preached. They were ready to accept me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1933. 1933-- '32-- '32-33. Then they said to me, we can't afford to give you a proper salary, but we'd like you. Because we would like you to become a member of the board of shechita so the-- the town itself will pay 1/2 your salary. I said, I don't mind, as long as it's arranged properly. By I won't take up a position in opposition to the board of shechita. I took, not an oath, I gave a solemn promise to the [NON-ENGLISH] and the chief Rabbi that I will not oppose them, not open-- what you call a-- a board of shechita in opposition. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Oh, oh, oh, the board of shechita of the town didn't accept me-- not me-- they didn't accept the congregation's plan. So I tried somewhere else. Suddenly, two years later, I get a letter-- will I accept the job. In my naivete, or whatever you like to call it, I took it for granted. It took them two years to solve the problem. So it didn't dawn on me I should ask any more questions. To my horror, and disgust, and embarrassment, and heartbreak, I said goodbye to St. Annes, signed up, as it were, in Leeds. The following week, the president [LAUGHS] said to me, I'm sorry. We haven't come to any conclusion with the board of shechita. You'll have to start an opposition shechita. I said oh, no. I said, I've burnt my boats. I'm engaged-- planning to get married-- didn't know what to do. Other members of the congregation were sympathetically inclined towards me, but-- then I-- I looked over the building. In the cellars, it were-- it was very large underground rooms. I said, I'll start a cheder. To start a cheder you need a cheder. So we had petitions, this, that, and the other. And we started the cheder. And that money came independently very well, and as I-- I conducted services and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Was there any-- this was what? 1935?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there rumblings of maybe more trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, as long as I was certain-- no-- no, they were satisfied. They were-- well, there were many people who were on my side-- didn't like the idea of an opposition shechita.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I meant were there rumblings of problems with the Germans at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yeah. Well, at first there was a lot of anti-Semitism. When we-- the kids went to cheder, often, I used to lead them out-- chasing these-- these anti-Semite kids from other schools. And yes, then-- then the years were 1933, '34. Yes, there were rumblings. Oh yes, then I got into the university. I was concentrating on that. I said, I've got to get my MA. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- so yeah, we did. Then there were rumblings and all that sort of thing. And interestingly enough-- if I can bring it in now-- in 1937-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --a-- a-- the great Zionist leader, Ze've Jabotinsky, a blessed memory, he was the progenitor of the [NON-ENGLISH] movement of now what they call [NON-ENGLISH] in Israel. And he addressed a meeting of 1,000 or more people in the cinema over in Chapeltown Road. And he said, amongst other things, of course, we've got to dump the Jews of Poland on the shores of Palestine, before it is too late. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In my naivete, if you like, again, I get up. I'm a member of the audience. I object to the speaker's terminology. You don't dump human beings. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He looked at me and said, I'm-- I'm-- I'm-- I'm sorry to upset the reverend gentleman. But I still say we've got to dump the Jews of Poland on the shores of Palestine before it's too late-- Polish. This was 1938. In 1945, for my sins, I went into Belsen. And one of the young men I met there, after I came to-- my senses were numbed, at least for minutes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then I start to hear Jabotinsky's voice-- and his advice, and his appeal. Then this-- then I start to talk to myself, apparently. It's very difficult to describe what-- what I went through mentally. He said, yes. He came to us, in 1936. And he said, don't learn the value of silks and satins. Learn the value of a gun-- how to defend yourselves. And get out. We ignored him. Many people considered him as a fascist. Many people-- the leaders-- consider him a troublemaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyhow-- so then-- then I was-- where I'm going to? I'm going back into-- into Belsen now. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then-- then we started-- then we started working, talking to people. I had nothing except my own hands and a-- and a mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Can we just go back before you get into--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the liberation bit? How did you get involved with the army? You-- you got your MA

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at Leeds.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, so when-- so-- so naturally, when war broke out-- just before war broke out, many people volunteered. And a lot of the young men of my community also volunteered.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you'd finished getting your M.A. at Leeds at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, I got that in 1940-- '41. And the funniest thing was when I was gonna go there to receive it, you know, in my cap and gown, and-- and the certificate, the order was it had to be canceled. The order came through that no large congregations or assemblies was to be permitted because of the freight of-- of the bombs. So war--

speaker: interviewer
text: So war had already broken out in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes-- '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was '41?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so they sent to me-- so they sent to me. Pity-- I missed that. It was quite an exciting assembly. Well, I mean to others-- wait a minute. Before then-- though mum and dad-- bless them-- were living in Liverpool at that time, as I told you. All these years, they'd been living there. That became a special area-- forbidden area-- for aliens. And they both had to get out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: My brother was-- my younger brother was in the army already. And then suddenly-- I wasn't in till 1942. I get a telephone call from dad, crying. I said what's the matter now? I've got to get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I stay with you? I have nowhere else to go. I was in trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dunkirk had taken place. And a certain unit-- of all those who managed to escape-- came around in the-- Hendon-- in the Leeds area. And I was asked as the minister of that particular area to see if I can get families to put the boys up until they were sent back to their units. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I took two soldiers. There were no more room for dad. Eventually, we got one taken to another house. And I-- and this-- and dad came to me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Interesting, that soldier eventually got married from my house. But anyhow, that's another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was your father in trouble, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Austrian. He was an enemy alien.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, right. So you've--

speaker: interviewee
text: He hadn't been naturalized.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1940 and '80 what was a war to end all wars? A country fit for heroes! Anyhow-- and he never bothered! Who bothered?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came. I went to shul, as I went every day. I come back about 20 past 8:00 in the morning. Two detectives are there. What's the matter now? We've come to arrest an' intern your father. Oh, my God. So I didn't know what to do. They wouldn't-- I said, give me a couple of hours. Let me think it over, see if I can get-- contact somebody. No, sir. Anyhow, they took him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then apparently, they had taken several others in the town. And everybody complained to their representative council-- you know, that's the provincial board of deputies. And so did I. And the late Professor Brodetsky, a blessed memory-- he was the president at that time. And he was a Leeds man. He came over. And he appealed to the community-- don't embarrass the government. I got angry. If it was your father, you wouldn't say it like that. You wouldn't speak like that. For somebody else-- there's a famous saying. It's good to shave on somebody else's face. In Yiddish, it's a stronger expression. It's good to [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. But anyhow, they appeared. So dad was taken to Highton in the first instance, and eventually to the Isle of Man.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went. So I said I will-- these Jews need also a chaplain there. Passover is coming. So I go to-- I was appointed as chaplain.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to the commanding officer there-- I don't think he realized I was the-- the son of one of them. I said, I need five or six men to come and carry the matzahs and all the other necessities there. He says OK, padre. Carry on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So dad stayed out with me for a few days. Then he went to-- the Isle of Man. And then this was what? '41. So you weren't in-- in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't in the army yet, no. My congregation refused to give me permission. They were worried. They said, what are they gonna do if they can't get another man? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to go in and help with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. This was a-- never mind what the fascist says-- a Jewish war-- to me it was a Jewish war-- not in that sense. And whether there-- there were insufficient-- insufficient troops there to cover whatever-- to warrant another padre I don't know. Or I might not have been of the high-ranking Jews in the sense. Only rabbis from Jewish college were considered to be fit chaplains. We disabused them afterwards. But anyhow, eventually, more and more boys were called up. They needed chaplains. Then they asked me to come. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so-- so I went to London. I was interviewed by Dayan Gollop, a blessed memory. He was the senior chaplain. And right. Then I got a letter-- please go to Chester on Saturday, so and so a date-- March, I think it was. I said, I'm sorry. I can't come. I said, I'll come Friday, if you like. I-- oh, yes-- I phoned up the commandant. He wasn't in. His wife was there. She says, OK. Well, you better come Friday.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I arrived there, I was the only fellow there. And she said, choose your own bedroom, which I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were there for 10 days, I think it was. And we learned how to salute, and one or two other bits and pieces-- but not the real gritty stuff for-- for-- for army life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the other men who arrived, they weren't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were-- they arrived on Saturday, because I was the only Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, they was chaplains, or they--

speaker: interviewee
text: All chaplains, yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --they were all chaplains. Yeah, we all had our uniforms at that time. And well, you had to have your uniform. They sent you a check for-- I don't know how much they come into Austin Reeds. It was the first time in my life I had a pair of wonderful shoes on my feet. At least the army paid. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes, quite interesting-- just two little points-- in spite of it being at terrible war-- and all the news was coming through of the atrocities in the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The commandant said at one-- at the breakfast, I'm going to have an extra portion of bacon this week. Everybody looked at him, and they didn't realize, whether I was Jewish. I can't remember. Oh, Hardman doesn't eat bacon. All right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we had a gas drill. And he said, remember. As soon as the bell rings, or whatever, the warning is, hold your breath and then put your masks on. At that fraction of a second, I couldn't think of the difference between holding your breath and breathing in. I breathed in. And he said Hardman's dead. Everybody laughed, and guffawed, and-- [LAUGHS] I think that stopped the-- the course for a few hours. Then I learned a wonderful moral lesson. In fact, the commandant told it to us. I was waiting in the street for a bus. And then an army truck came by, and I hailed it. He stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me a lift? The fellow was a private. He said sure, sir. He was a lieutenant colonel, so. And as we're going down the hill, he was telling us-- he went into-- what's the expression? Into neutral, which is forbidden. And he told him it's forbidden. He said, yes, sir, I know it's forbidden. I'm ready to be put on a charge. But why are you doing it? I've got a brother in the merchant navy, and he's bringing over the oil and petrol, et cetera. And he was thinking of him. And the order, you know, that came through that the-- some of our-- our ships are being sunk. So that was a moral lesson. Right-- so what-- what happened? Well, eventually we-- oh, then one or two of them were quite scholarly in Hebraic matters. And we had a little argument, a discussion, rather, on the grammatical point-- a grammatical point. So I said let's-- let's solve it by going to Manchester. It's only a 1/2 an hour's run or so. And I know Professor Manson of the Old Testament Department. We arrived that Sunday afternoon, and he looked at us. He said, in the middle of a war, [LAUGHS] you guys are worried about a small grammatical point in the Hebrew language? And he thought it was lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we all-- then we disappeared to our own particular units. Then I was sent to East Central District.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dunstable. Dunstable. And then there were three messes-- one for the general, and lieutenant colonels, and colonels, and one or two majors. And then the second was lieutenant colonels, and majors, and captains. And the third one was majors, captains, lieutenants, etc cetera. And I was in the third mess. But they said you got to find your own billet-- your own sleeping apartment. So I found a house not very, very far away, a home, of Mr. And Mrs. Shod. Catholics, they were. They were very sweet, very kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, I'll only be sleeping here-- no food. The only thing is you can give me my-- you can do my laundry. So I-- I gave laundry. And then the following night it came back. I said, so quick? And I gave them my-- oh no, stop-- I give them my pair of tzitzits to wash. And oh, the tzitzits came back the same night. I said, where's the rest, Mrs. Shod? Where's the rest of the laundry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a feeling that this particular item was a religious matter, and I didn't want the snotty girl-- snotty-nosed girls of the laundry to have anything to do that. I washed it myself-- very sweet of her.
